title: League of Dungeoneers - questing and creating your own persistent world
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sVwAWTxKDJ0

Daniel (@dungeondive) maps out the full quest structure of League of Dungeoneers, a question that puzzled him at the prototype stage. The answer: this is a sandbox of roughly fifty quests rather than a campaign-driven game — and that distinction reframes everything.

The all-in package contains the introductory First Blood skirmish, a six-quest opening campaign (The Dead Rising), the three-level Layer of the Spider Queen, thirteen random quests keyed to five objective rooms, six layered side quests, twelve mini-quests for travel, and a second quest book with the multi-dungeon Halls of the Goblin King, the Cult of the Hydra, the Witch Hunt, plus nineteen backer-contributed standalones. The Dark Pyramid expansion adds a desert mini-campaign in the southern Ancient Lands.

Daniel's frame: League of Dungeoneers is sword-and-sorcery (short stories and novellas), not epic fantasy. The character's arc never ends — campaigns are episodes within an open-ended hero survival game. He sketches a house rule for tying mercenaries to home settlements, generating emergent quests where you travel across the map looking for a specific NPC. The kind of persistent-world play that the system invites without demanding.

----

Sword-and-sorcery short stories or epic-fantasy novel arcs — which structure do you prefer in a dungeon crawler?
